question: [File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/repository/ContractRepository.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: findRuntimeBytecode()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger a stale-cache hit on findRuntimeBytecode() for a staking-contract whose bytecode was upgraded, causing the EVM to execute old reward-distribution logic and redirect unpaid staking rewards to an attacker-controlled address?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/repository/ContractRepository.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: findRuntimeBytecode()] Can an unprivileged user craft an eth_call targeting a contract that is a staking entity (type=CONTRACT, stakedNodeId != -1) and exploit the Caffeine cache's lack of TTL-based invalidation on staking-period boundaries to read and act on a stale pending_reward value, leading to double-claiming of unpaid staking rewards?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/repository/ContractRepository.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: findRuntimeBytecode()] Can an unprivileged user supply a contractId that resolves to the staking reward account (0.0.800) in findRuntimeBytecode(), causing the EVM to load and execute arbitrary bytecode associated with that system account and siphon unpaid staking rewards?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/repository/ContractRepository.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: findRuntimeBytecode()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the unless = '#result == null' cache condition in findRuntimeBytecode() by first triggering a null-result cache miss for a staking contract, then racing a second call that loads attacker-controlled bytecode before the legitimate bytecode is cached, causing incorrect staking reward distribution?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/repository/ContractRepository.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: findRuntimeBytecode()] Can an unprivileged user send a high volume of eth_call requests for a staking contract to exhaust the Caffeine cache capacity configured via cacheProperties.getContract(), forcing eviction of legitimate bytecode entries and causing the EVM to re-fetch stale or incorrect bytecode that misroutes staking rewards?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/repository/ContractRepository.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: findRuntimeBytecode()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the fact that findRuntimeBytecode() uses a native SQL query (select runtime_bytecode from contract where id = :contractId) without timestamp filtering to retrieve bytecode from a deleted or replaced staking contract, executing logic that incorrectly claims pending staking rewards?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/repository/ContractRepository.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: findRuntimeBytecode()] Can an unprivileged user pass a negative or zero contractId to findRuntimeBytecode() to bypass the contract lookup and trigger an unexpected code path in the EVM that mishandles staking reward accounting for contracts with pending rewards?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/repository/ContractRepository.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: findRuntimeBytecode()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the absence of access control on findRuntimeBytecode() to enumerate all contract IDs, identify contracts that are staking entities with large pending_reward values, and then craft EVM calls that exploit their cached bytecode to redirect those rewards?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/repository/ContractRepository.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: findRuntimeBytecode()] Can an unprivileged user trigger a cache race condition in findRuntimeBytecode() by sending concurrent requests for the same staking contract ID, causing two threads to simultaneously load different bytecode versions and execute conflicting staking reward logic that results in double-payment of pending rewards?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/repository/ContractRepository.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: findRuntimeBytecode()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the fact that findRuntimeBytecode() caches Optional<byte[]> results and the cache key is only the contractId (Long) to perform a cache key collision attack where two different contract entities share the same numeric ID across shards/realms, causing the wrong bytecode to be executed for a staking contract and misrouting its pending rewards?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/repository/ContractRepository.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: findRuntimeBytecode()] Can an unprivileged user deploy a contract that mimics the interface of a staking reward precompile, then exploit the cache in findRuntimeBytecode() to substitute its bytecode for the legitimate precompile bytecode, causing staking reward transfers to be redirected to the attacker's address?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/repository/ContractRepository.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: findRuntimeBytecode()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the CrudRepository-inherited findById() method exposed by ContractRepository to read the full Contract entity (including initcode) for a staking contract, extract sensitive reward-routing logic, and craft a targeted exploit to steal unpaid staking rewards?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/repository/ContractRepository.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: findRuntimeBytecode()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the fact that findRuntimeBytecode() does not validate whether the returned bytecode belongs to a contract that has declineReward=false and stakedNodeId != -1, allowing execution of arbitrary bytecode in the context of a staking entity and theft of its pending rewards?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/repository/ContractRepository.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: findRuntimeBytecode()] Can an unprivileged user trigger a cache poisoning attack by first causing findRuntimeBytecode() to cache an empty byte array for a staking contract's ID, then exploiting the unless = '#result == null' condition (which only skips caching for null, not empty arrays) to permanently serve empty bytecode, preventing legitimate reward-claiming transactions from executing?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/repository/ContractRepository.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: findRuntimeBytecode()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the lack of timestamp-aware caching in findRuntimeBytecode() to perform a historical eth_call at a block where a staking contract had different reward-routing bytecode, causing the mirror node to return incorrect staking reward data that can be used to claim rewards already paid?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/repository/ContractRepository.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: findRuntimeBytecode()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the shared Caffeine cache (CACHE_NAME_CONTRACT) between historical and current eth_call requests in findRuntimeBytecode() to serve historical bytecode for a current staking contract call, executing outdated reward-distribution logic that allows theft of pending staking rewards?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/repository/ContractRepository.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: findRuntimeBytecode()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the fact that findRuntimeBytecode() returns Optional<byte[]> and the cache stores the raw byte array, allowing a crafted contractId to return bytecode containing SELFDESTRUCT or reward-redirecting opcodes that drain the staking reward account (0.0.800) during EVM execution?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/repository/ContractRepository.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: findRuntimeBytecode()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the absence of bytecode integrity verification in findRuntimeBytecode() to cause the EVM to execute tampered bytecode for a staking contract, where the tampered code calls the staking precompile to redirect pending rewards to an attacker-controlled account?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/repository/ContractRepository.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: findRuntimeBytecode()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the CrudRepository.count() method inherited by ContractRepository to infer the number of deployed contracts and correlate with entity_stake records to identify high-value staking contracts, then target those contracts for reward theft via crafted EVM calls?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/repository/ContractRepository.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: findRuntimeBytecode()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the fact that findRuntimeBytecode() caches results indefinitely (until eviction) without checking if the contract's staking configuration (stakedNodeId, declineReward) has changed, allowing execution of stale reward-claiming logic after a contract has opted out of staking rewards?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/repository/ContractRepository.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: findRuntimeBytecode()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the native SQL query in findRuntimeBytecode() (select runtime_bytecode from contract where id = :contractId) by providing a contractId that maps to a system contract (e.g., the HTS precompile address), causing the EVM to execute system-level logic that bypasses staking reward access controls?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/repository/ContractRepository.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: findRuntimeBytecode()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the Long type of contractId in findRuntimeBytecode() to pass a Long.MAX_VALUE or Long.MIN_VALUE, causing integer overflow in the underlying SQL query and returning bytecode for an unintended contract that has accumulated staking rewards?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/repository/ContractRepository.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: findRuntimeBytecode()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the fact that findRuntimeBytecode() does not differentiate between a contract that is actively staking and one that has been deleted (deleted=true in entity table), causing the EVM to execute bytecode for a deleted staking contract and claim its accumulated but unpaid rewards?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/repository/ContractRepository.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: findRuntimeBytecode()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the Caffeine cache's time-based eviction policy in CACHE_MANAGER_CONTRACT to time an attack precisely when a staking contract's bytecode is evicted from cache, injecting a request that causes the re-fetched bytecode to be from a different (attacker-controlled) contract row, leading to staking reward theft?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/repository/ContractRepository.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: findRuntimeBytecode()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the fact that findRuntimeBytecode() is called by ContractBytecodeReadableKVState without verifying the contract's entity type, allowing a non-CONTRACT entity (e.g., ACCOUNT) to have its bytecode loaded and executed in a context that grants access to staking reward balances?
